In the United Kingdom, [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_unions "Trade unions") emerged in the early 19th century, but faced punitive laws that sharply limited their activities. They began political activity in the late 19th century and formed an alliance with the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") in the early 20th century. They grew rapidly from 1900 to 1920, lost their legal disabilities, and were well established by the 1920s. Union members largely switched from Liberal to the new [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). Its leader [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald") became prime minister in 1924 briefly, and then again in 1929. In the 1980s [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher")'s Conservative governments weakened the powers of the unions by making it more difficult to strike legally. Most British unions are members of the TUC, the [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") (founded in 1867), or where appropriate, the [Scottish Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Scottish_Trades_Union_Congress "Scottish Trades Union Congress") or the [Irish Congress of Trade Unions](/wiki/Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions "Irish Congress of Trade Unions"), which are the country's principal [national trade union centres](/wiki/National_trade_union_center "National trade union center").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of trade unions in the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom "History of trade unions in the United Kingdom")

Trade unions in the United Kingdom were first decriminalised under the recommendation of a [Royal commission](/wiki/Royal_commission "Royal commission") in 1867, which agreed that the establishment of the organisations was to the advantage of both employers and employees. Legalised in 1871, the [Trade Union Movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement") sought to reform socio-economic conditions for working men in British industries, and the [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union")' search for this led to the creation of a [Labour Representation Committee](/wiki/Labour_Representation_Committee_(1900) "Labour Representation Committee (1900)") which effectively formed the basis for today's [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), which still has extensive links with the Trade Union Movement in Britain.

As factories closed, membership declined steeply in the 1980s and 1990s, falling from 13 million in 1979 to around 7.3 million in 2000. In September 2012 union membership dropped below 6 million for the first time since the 1940s.[[4]](#cite_note-2012_membership-4) Union membership has since begun rising gradually again, reaching 6.44 million in 2019.

## Law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Law")]

Further information: [United Kingdom labour law](/wiki/United_Kingdom_labour_law "United Kingdom labour law")

Much like [corporations](/wiki/UK_company_law "UK company law"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) trade unions were regarded as criminal until the [Combination Act 1825](/wiki/Combination_Act_1825 "Combination Act 1825"), and were regarded as quasi-legal organisations, subjected to the [restraint of trade](/wiki/Restraint_of_trade "Restraint of trade") doctrine, until the [Trade Union Act 1871](/wiki/Trade_Union_Act_1871 "Trade Union Act 1871"). This Act abolished common-law restrictions, but took an abstentionist stance to unions' internal affairs. The [Trade Disputes Act 1906](/wiki/Trade_Disputes_Act_1906 "Trade Disputes Act 1906") exempted trade-union funds from liability in action for damages for torts, and this freedom gave future union pickets a great deal of power.

### Democratic organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Democratic organisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Unison_strike_rally_Oxford_20060328.jpg/250px-Unison_strike_rally_Oxford_20060328.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unison_strike_rally_Oxford_20060328.jpg)

2006 rally by [Unison](/wiki/Unison_(trade_union) "Unison (trade union)"), the largest trade union in the United Kingdom[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The principle that the common law enforced a union's own rules, and that unions were free to arrange their affairs, is reflected in the [ILO Freedom of Association Convention](/wiki/ILO_Freedom_of_Association_Convention "ILO Freedom of Association Convention") and in [Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/Article_11_of_the_European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights"), subject to the requirement that regulations "[necessary in a democratic society](/wiki/Necessary_in_a_democratic_society "Necessary in a democratic society")" may be imposed. Unions must have an executive body and that executive must, under the [Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992](/wiki/Trade_Union_and_Labour_Relations_(Consolidation)_Act_1992 "Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992") sections 46 to 56, be elected at least every five years, directly in a secret, equal [postal vote](/wiki/Postal_vote "Postal vote") of union members.

### Union constitutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Union constitutions")]

The structure of the unions was based in [contract](/wiki/English_contract_law "English contract law"), and the rights of members depended on being able to show some [proprietary](/wiki/English_property_law "English property law") interest to be [specifically enforced](/wiki/Specifically_enforced "Specifically enforced").[[7]](#cite_note-7) This meant that the express terms of the union rule book can, like any contract, be supplemented with implied terms by the courts as strictly necessary to reflect the reasonable expectations of the parties,[[8]](#cite_note-8) for instance, by implying the [Electoral Reform Service](/wiki/Electoral_Reform_Service "Electoral Reform Service")'s guidance to say what happens in a tie break situation during an election when the union rules are silent.[[9]](#cite_note-9) If there are irregular occurrences in the affairs of the union, for instance if negligence or mismanagement is not alleged and a majority could vote on the issue to forgive them, then members have no individual rights to contest executive decision making.[[10]](#cite_note-10) However, if a union's leadership acts *[ultra vires](/wiki/Ultra_vires "Ultra vires")*, beyond its powers set out in the union constitution, if the alleged wrongdoers are in control, if a special supra-majority procedure is flouted, or a member's personal right is broken, the members may bring a derivative claim in court to sue or restrain the executive members. So in *[Edwards v Halliwell](/wiki/Edwards_v_Halliwell "Edwards v Halliwell")*[[11]](#cite_note-11) a decision of the executive committee of the [National Union of Vehicle Builders](/wiki/National_Union_of_Vehicle_Builders "National Union of Vehicle Builders") to increase membership fees, which were set in the constitution and required a two-thirds majority vote, was able to be restrained by a claim from individual members because this touched both a personal right under the constitution and flouted a special procedure.

### Discipline and expulsion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Discipline and expulsion")]

* *[ASLEF v United Kingdom](/wiki/ASLEF_v_United_Kingdom "ASLEF v United Kingdom")* [2007] ECHR 184
* *[McVitae v UNISON](/w/index.php?title=McVitae_v_UNISON&action=edit&redlink=1 "McVitae v UNISON (page does not exist)")* [1996] IRLR 33

### Subscriptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Subscriptions")]

Members' subscriptions are often paid by DOCAS (Deduction of Contributions at Source) i.e. deduction from salary. Implementation of the draft Trade Union (Deduction of Union Subscriptions from Wages in the Public Sector) Regulations 2017 has been delayed until 2019.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Union organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Union organisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Wanja_Lundby-Wedin.jpg/250px-Wanja_Lundby-Wedin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wanja_Lundby-Wedin.jpg)

The [ETUC](/wiki/ETUC "ETUC"), headed by [Wanja Lundby-Wedin](/wiki/Wanja_Lundby-Wedin "Wanja Lundby-Wedin") until May 2011, is the union federation for 37 European countries, the counterpart for the [TUC](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") of the UK and the [ITUC](/wiki/ITUC "ITUC") internationally.

[Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher")'s Conservative government, first elected in 1979, saw trade unions as an obstacle to economic growth and passed legislation of the sort the Conservatives had mostly long avoided.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Membership declined steeply in the 1980s and 1990s, falling from 13 million in 1979 to around 7.3 million in 2000. In 2012, union membership dropped below 6 million for the first time since the 1940s.[[4]](#cite_note-2012_membership-4) From 1980 to 1998, the proportion of employees who were union members fell from 52 per cent to 30 per cent.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Union membership declined in parallel with the reduction in size of many traditional industries which had been highly unionised, such as steel, coal, printing, and the docks.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2016, the Conservative government passed a new [Trade Union Act](/wiki/Trade_Union_Act_2016 "Trade Union Act 2016"), which proposes stricter ballot thresholds for industrial action, further restraints on picketing and a requirement that union members contributions to political funds would only be via an ‘opt-in’.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
